citation: Cosio PL, Moreno-Simonet L, Fernández D, Lloret M, Padulles X, Padulles JM, Farran-Codina A, Rodas G, Cadefau JA. Football (soccer) match-derived hamstring muscles residual fatigue can be monitored using early rate of torque development. Eur J Appl Physiol. 2024. doi:10.1007/s00421-024-05694-x
abstract: This study investigated whether early rate of torque development (RTD) is more sensitive than maximal voluntary isometric contraction (MVIC) torque for detecting post-match hamstring fatigue in soccer players. Nineteen male regional first-division soccer players (age 20.9±2.0 years) underwent repeated measures assessments at pre-match (MD), MD+24h, MD+48h, and MD+72h using an isometric prone curl test (IPC; 90° hip, 20° knee flexion). RTD was measured at 0–50 ms (RTD0-50), 0–100 ms (RTD0-100), 0–250 ms (RTD0-250), and peak RTD. Serum biomarkers assessed concurrently included creatine kinase (CK), sarcomeric mitochondrial CK (sMtCK), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), malondialdehyde (MDA), and irisin. Results showed that while MVIC torque recovered to baseline by MD+72h, early RTD indices did not: RTD0-50 showed a large significant decrement (p<0.001, g=−1.24) and RTD0-100 remained depressed (p<0.001, g=−1.06) at MD+72h. All serum biomarkers remained elevated through MD+72h, indicating ongoing muscle damage and inflammation beyond the point of perceived MVIC recovery. RTD0-250 and RTDpeak recovered by MD+72h. The study concludes that early RTD windows (0–50 ms and 0–100 ms) detect residual neuromuscular fatigue that MVIC torque misses, and that 72 hours is insufficient for complete hamstring recovery following a competitive soccer match.
tldr: Standard isometric strength testing (MVIC) suggests hamstrings recover within 72 hours after a soccer match, but early rate of torque development (RTD0-50 and RTD0-100) reveals that explosive force capacity remains significantly impaired at MD+72h. This means players may appear 'strong' on conventional strength tests while still carrying meaningful neuromuscular fatigue. Serum biomarkers corroborate ongoing muscle stress. For return-to-play and load management decisions, RTD-based testing provides a more sensitive window into true hamstring readiness than MVIC alone.
methods: Single-group repeated measures design; n=19 male regional first-division soccer players (age 20.9±2.0y, height 178.3±5.5cm, mass 73.4±7.6kg). Isometric prone curl (IPC) test at 90° hip flexion and 20° knee flexion using a custom dynamometer. RTD computed over 0–50 ms, 0–100 ms, 0–250 ms intervals and peak RTD. Assessments at pre-match (MD), MD+24h, MD+48h, MD+72h. Venous blood draws at same time points for CK, sMtCK, ALT, AST, MDA, and irisin. Statistical analysis: repeated measures ANOVA with Hedges' g effect sizes; significance set at p<0.05.
findings: RTD0-50 was significantly reduced at all post-match time points versus baseline and remained unrecovered at MD+72h (p<0.001, g=−1.24). RTD0-100 similarly unrecovered at MD+72h (p<0.001, g=−1.06). RTD0-250 and RTDpeak recovered by MD+72h. MVIC torque recovered by MD+72h. CK, sMtCK, ALT, AST, MDA, and irisin were all elevated through MD+72h. Early RTD indices (0–50 ms and 0–100 ms) were significantly more sensitive markers of residual hamstring fatigue than MVIC torque, detecting neuromuscular deficits that MVIC testing did not capture.
limitations: Small sample (n=19) from a single regional division; all male; no control group; individual match demands not controlled; no imaging to confirm absence of structural injury; ecological validity limited as testing context differs from in-game contractions; reliability of IPC test at very early RTD windows not independently validated in this population.
practicalImplications: Practitioners should not rely solely on MVIC torque to clear hamstrings post-match; RTD0-50 and RTD0-100 provide a more sensitive readiness marker. A 72-hour recovery window is likely insufficient for full explosive hamstring capacity restoration. Incorporating early RTD assessment into weekly monitoring can better inform training load decisions, particularly regarding sprint and high-intensity work scheduled 3 days post-match. Serum biomarkers (CK, sMtCK) can complement RTD data as objective indicators of ongoing muscle stress.
athleteDev: For football players at all levels, this research underscores that hamstring explosive capacity — critical for sprinting and change of direction — lags behind maximal strength recovery after high-demand competition. Athlete development programs should include RTD-sensitive testing tools and build sufficient recovery windows (>72h) before high-velocity sprint sessions following competitive play. Monitoring early RTD across a season may help identify cumulative fatigue patterns before injury risk escalates.
rtp: Following hamstring strain injury, RTD0-50 and RTD0-100 indices should be incorporated alongside MVIC assessments in return-to-play criteria. An athlete demonstrating full MVIC recovery but deficient early RTD has not fully restored explosive neuromuscular function, which is directly implicated in re-injury during sprinting. Serum biomarkers provide a complementary systemic indicator of ongoing muscle damage. Clearance decisions should include early RTD symmetry thresholds, not only strength-based measures.

citation: de Oliveira DM, Silva AKAC, Macedo AG, Fernandes MB, Fernandes EV. Global research trends in sports nutrition and football over the last 20 years (2004–2024). Sports. 2025;13(10):365. doi:10.3390/sports13100365
abstract: This scientometric analysis mapped the global landscape of sports nutrition research applied to football (soccer) from 2004 to 2024. The authors retrieved 526 original articles from Web of Science, PubMed, and Scopus, analyzing publication outputs, citations, author contributions, institutional and country affiliations, journal distribution, and keyword co-occurrence networks using Bibliometrix v5.0.1 and VOSviewer v1.6.20. Publication volume grew by approximately 1,450% over the study period. The UK was the dominant contributing country (46.6% of publications), with Morton J and Maughan R identified as the most prolific authors. The journal Nutrients accounted for 29.6% of all publications. Keyword trend analysis revealed temporal shifts in research focus: early work (2004–2014) concentrated on hydration and carbohydrate intake, while more recent literature (2015–2024) has pivoted toward low energy availability (LEA), polyphenols, antioxidants, and gut microbiome. Emerging and underexplored topics include oxidative stress, beta-alanine supplementation, and the consequences of LEA in football populations. The findings provide a structured overview of where football nutrition science has been and where productive research gaps remain.
tldr: A 20-year bibliometric analysis of football sports nutrition research reveals explosive growth in the field (1,450% increase in publications), a dominant UK research presence, and a clear thematic shift from hydration/carbohydrate focus toward low energy availability, polyphenols, and gut health. Practitioners benefit from knowing which nutritional topics have strong evidence bases (hydration, CHO) versus emerging areas requiring more research (LEA, beta-alanine, oxidative stress). This map helps prioritize evidence-based nutrition strategies while flagging where caution and continued monitoring of new literature are warranted.
methods: Scientometric analysis. Database search: Web of Science, PubMed, and Scopus; 526 original articles; date range 2004–2024; search terms related to sports nutrition AND football/soccer. Bibliometric tools: Bibliometrix v5.0.1 (R package) for performance analysis and science mapping; VOSviewer v1.6.20 for keyword co-occurrence network visualization. Analyses included: annual publication trends, citation metrics, h-index by author, country and institutional collaboration networks, source journal analysis, keyword trend mapping (time-sliced co-occurrence).
findings: Publication volume increased ~1,450% from 2004 to 2024. The UK contributed 46.6% of all publications; Morton J and Maughan R were the most prolific individual authors. Nutrients was the top publishing journal (29.6% of articles). Early-period dominant keywords: hydration, carbohydrate, energy. Late-period emerging keywords: low energy availability, polyphenols, antioxidants, gut microbiome. Underexplored topics with low publication density but rising interest: oxidative stress, beta-alanine, LEA consequences in male and female football players. Co-authorship networks revealed concentrated UK–European collaboration clusters with limited South American and Asian representation despite large football populations in those regions.
limitations: Scientometric analyses describe publication patterns, not evidence quality; high-volume research areas may not reflect the highest-impact interventions. Keyword co-occurrence reflects author-chosen terminology which can vary across regions and time. Restricting to three databases may exclude regional journals. The 20-year window captures maturation of the field but cannot assess causality of trend drivers (e.g., whether publication growth reflects practitioner demand or funding availability).
practicalImplications: Applied sport scientists and nutritionists working in football can use this landscape map to identify the robustness of evidence underpinning common practices (hydration and CHO protocols have decades of support) versus newer strategies (LEA management, polyphenol supplementation) that require more individualized and cautious application. Awareness of underexplored topics (beta-alanine, oxidative stress) flags areas where practitioners should seek emerging trial data rather than relying on established protocols from other sports.
athleteDev: For long-term athlete development in football, the evidence landscape suggests carbohydrate periodization and hydration protocols remain the bedrock nutritional interventions. Emerging research on LEA is particularly relevant for youth and female athletes, where energy balance monitoring should be integrated into development programs. Polyphenol and antioxidant strategies show growing research interest, warranting consideration for recovery optimization in development pipelines.
rtp: Nutritional support during return-to-play is an area with limited specific research identified in this analysis. The established literature on carbohydrate and protein intake for muscle repair provides the primary evidence base. Practitioners should monitor emerging LEA and antioxidant/polyphenol literature for applications in injury recovery and tissue healing protocols, recognizing these areas are still accumulating evidence.

citation: Luxenburg D, Wasserman N, Geller J, Perez J, Burke J, Kaplan L. Hamstring injuries in the National Football League: An epidemiological study. J Orthop. 2022. doi:10.1016/j.jor.2022.11.010
abstract: This retrospective epidemiological study characterized the incidence, position-specific risk, surface type associations, and recurrence patterns of hamstring injuries in the NFL across the 2013–2016 regular seasons. Using official NFL gamebooks, the authors identified 78 documented hamstring injuries across 64 regular-season games per season. Standardized incidence ratios (SIR) were calculated by position group to determine relative risk compared to the overall population. Linebackers (LB) demonstrated the highest SIR (2.02), followed by defensive backs (DB, SIR=1.62), while offensive linemen (OL, SIR=0.28) and defensive linemen (DL, SIR=0.29) were at substantially lower risk. The majority of injuries (57.7%) occurred on artificial turf, though this difference did not reach statistical significance (p=0.082). No effect of short rest (e.g., Thursday games) on injury rate was identified (p=0.959). Mean injury week was 8.05 (median 7.5), suggesting a mid-season concentration. A recurrence rate of 10.3% was observed within the study period. The study highlights that speed/skill positions with high sprint demands carry the greatest hamstring injury risk in professional American football.
tldr: In the NFL from 2013–2016, hamstring injuries disproportionately affect linebackers (2× expected rate) and defensive backs (1.6× expected rate), reflecting the high-speed sprint demands of those positions. Offensive and defensive linemen have markedly lower risk. Injuries cluster in mid-season (around week 8) and recur in roughly 1 in 10 cases. Artificial turf did not significantly increase risk in this sample. These patterns have direct relevance for position-specific screening, load management, and targeted prevention programming in American football.
methods: Retrospective epidemiological analysis. Data source: official NFL gamebooks from the 2013–2016 regular seasons; 78 hamstring injuries identified across 256 regular-season games per season. Standardized incidence ratios (SIR) calculated per position group (QB, RB, WR, TE, OL, LB, DB, DL) with 95% confidence intervals. Chi-squared tests used to compare injury rates on natural grass versus artificial turf. Z-test used to compare injury rates in short-rest game weeks (Thursday following Sunday) versus standard rest. Recurrence defined as same-player same-injury type within the study period.
findings: 78 hamstring injuries documented across 2013–2016 regular seasons. LB SIR=2.02 (highest among position groups); DB SIR=1.62; WR and RB moderate SIRs; OL SIR=0.28; DL SIR=0.29. 57.7% of injuries on artificial turf vs. 42.3% on natural grass (p=0.082, not significant). No significant short-rest effect (p=0.959). Mean injury week 8.05 (median 7.5). Recurrence rate 10.3% within study period. Position groups with high sprint and change-of-direction demands showed the greatest injury burden.
limitations: Gamebooks may underreport injuries that do not result in game absence; injuries resulting only in practice missed would not be captured. Study period (2013–2016) may not reflect current injury trends due to rule changes, training evolution, and surface upgrades. Sample limited to regular season; preseason and postseason data excluded. No severity grading (MRI-based classification) available from gamebook records. Confounding variables (age, prior injury history, training load) not controlled.
practicalImplications: NFL strength and conditioning and sports medicine staff should prioritize hamstring injury prevention resources toward linebackers and defensive backs, who face roughly 2× and 1.6× the expected injury rate respectively. Mid-season monitoring (weeks 7–10) warrants heightened vigilance given injury clustering at this time point. The 10.3% recurrence rate underscores the importance of structured return-to-sport criteria and post-clearance monitoring. Turf type showed a non-significant trend toward higher injury rates that may warrant further investigation with larger samples.
athleteDev: For American football player development, position-specific sprint and acceleration profiles should drive hamstring training and screening priorities. Linebackers and defensive backs — who execute the most high-velocity sprints in game conditions — require robust hamstring strength, eccentric capacity, and neuromuscular control programming throughout development. Early-career load monitoring during the mid-season phase (weeks 7–10) may help reduce primary injury incidence at peak risk windows.
rtp: Given the 10.3% within-season recurrence rate identified in this NFL sample, return-to-play protocols for hamstring injuries in American football must include recurrence prevention as a primary endpoint. Position-specific sprint demands — particularly for LBs and DBs — should inform functional clearance criteria. Rehabilitation should progress to position-specific high-speed running and cutting before game clearance. Ongoing load monitoring post-RTP is warranted through the mid-season period when re-injury risk is historically concentrated.

citation: Valente HG, de Oliveira RR, Baroni BM. How are hamstring strain injuries managed in elite men's football clubs? A survey with 62 Brazilian physical therapists. Phys Ther Sport. 2023;61:73–81. doi:10.1016/j.ptsp.2023.03.001
abstract: This cross-sectional survey examined hamstring strain injury (HSI) assessment and rehabilitation practices among physical therapists (PTs) at elite Brazilian men's football clubs. Sixty-two PTs from 35 of 40 eligible clubs (87.5% representativeness) from Brazil's Series A and B divisions (2021 season) completed a 30-question online questionnaire covering injury classification, imaging use, rehabilitation structure, therapeutic modalities, and return-to-play (RTP) criteria. All 62 respondents (100%) used imaging for HSI assessment and rated it as 'important' or 'very important.' Injury classification was universally applied, with 45.2% using the Munich Consensus Classification. Rehabilitation programs were most commonly structured in 3–4 phases. Universal or near-universal adoption was observed for electrophysical agents (100%), strengthening exercises (98.4%, with 93.5% using eccentric protocols), manual therapy (96.8%), football-specific exercises (95.2%), and lumbopelvic stabilization (93.5%). Muscle strength was rated the most important RTP criterion by 71% of respondents. The survey reveals high convergence around core HSI management practices in elite Brazilian football, with eccentric strengthening and imaging-guided classification forming the bedrock of contemporary rehabilitation.
tldr: Among 62 physical therapists managing hamstring injuries at Brazil's top professional football clubs, there is near-universal consensus on the core elements of treatment: imaging for all cases, injury classification (Munich Consensus most common), eccentric strengthening, electrophysical agents, manual therapy, and football-specific rehabilitation. Muscle strength is the dominant RTP criterion. This snapshot of elite practice in one of the world's most football-intensive countries confirms that evidence-based principles are widely adopted, while also highlighting that subjective and functional RTP criteria vary more than objective strength measures.
methods: Cross-sectional online survey. Population: physical therapists from elite Brazilian men's football clubs (Series A and B, 2021 season); 35 of 40 eligible clubs represented (87.5%); n=62 PTs. Instrument: 30-question questionnaire developed by the research team covering HSI assessment (imaging, classification systems), rehabilitation structure (phases, progressions), therapeutic modalities (electrophysical agents, manual therapy, strengthening types, sport-specific exercises), and RTP criteria (strength testing, functional milestones, imaging clearance). Descriptive statistics reported as frequencies and percentages. No inferential statistics used; descriptive survey design.
findings: 100% of PTs used imaging for HSI assessment; 100% rated imaging 'important' or 'very important.' 100% used injury classification systems; Munich Consensus used by 45.2%. Most common rehabilitation structure: 3–4 phases. 100% used electrophysical agents. 98.4% used strengthening exercises; 93.5% used eccentric protocols specifically. 96.8% used manual therapy. 95.2% incorporated football-specific exercises. 93.5% used lumbopelvic stabilization. Muscle strength was ranked #1 RTP criterion by 71% of respondents. Variation was noted in the specific tools used for strength testing and in the precise functional milestones applied for RTP clearance.
limitations: Survey design captures reported practices, not actual implementation fidelity; social desirability bias may inflate adoption of evidence-based techniques. Restricted to Brazilian Series A and B; findings may not generalize to other football cultures or resource settings. No data on outcomes (re-injury rates, time-loss) linked to specific practices. Single-season snapshot (2021) may not reflect current or evolving practices. The survey did not distinguish between injury severity grades in practice patterns.
practicalImplications: Practitioners managing HSIs in football can benchmark their protocols against this large-sample elite survey: imaging-guided classification, eccentric strengthening, and multi-modal rehabilitation (manual therapy, electrophysical agents, lumbopelvic stabilization) are near-universal standards in elite settings. Muscle strength testing as the primary RTP criterion is supported by the majority of elite practitioners. Programs lacking eccentric strengthening, formal injury classification, or football-specific exercise progressions may fall below the standard of care observed in elite Brazilian football.
athleteDev: For football player development, this survey reinforces that comprehensive HSI management — not just isolated strengthening — includes lumbopelvic stabilization, sport-specific movement progression, and structured phase-based rehabilitation. Integrating these elements into development academy injury protocols, even at sub-elite levels, aligns practice with elite standards and may reduce re-injury rates. Developing PTs and athletic trainers should be trained in the Munich Consensus classification system to improve communication and benchmarking.
rtp: The dominance of muscle strength as the primary RTP criterion (71% of elite Brazilian PTs) reflects current evidence favoring objective strength benchmarks over symptom-based clearance. However, the survey also shows that football-specific exercises (95.2%) and functional milestones are widely integrated alongside strength testing, suggesting a multi-criteria RTP framework is standard in elite practice. Rehabilitation programs should incorporate progressive sport-specific loading and lumbopelvic stability, with clearance requiring both strength and functional competency milestones.
